title: Humanitarian Situation & Survival Map
subtitle: Verified humanitarian information — not predictive intelligence

links.resources: Find water, food, medical & shelter by area

lite_mode.map_disabled: Map disabled in lite mode to save data. Use Resources to browse by area.

ethics_banner: Humanitarian and educational support only. We do not predict events, designate safe zones, or provide threat forecasts. Discourse metrics measure published language — not operational risk. Information may be incomplete or delayed.
ethics_banner_short: Humanitarian info only — not threat forecasts

resources.title: Humanitarian Resources
resources.subtitle: Browse by area — sorted by last update. Verify locally before visiting.
resources.verify_locally: Verify this location is still operating before travelling.

hotlines.title: Hotlines & Contacts
hotlines.subtitle: Published humanitarian and emergency numbers — verify locally
hotlines.verify_note: Phone numbers and services change. Always verify locally. Tap to call where supported.

survival.title: Survival Guides
survival.subtitle: General humanitarian education — not location-specific safety advice
survival.offline_note: This content is cached for offline access

water.title: Water Safety
items: Boil water for at least 1 minute if possible before drinking
Use cloth filtration to remove visible particles when boiling is not possible
Avoid water from unknown sources; contamination risks include bacteria and chemicals
Rainwater collection: use clean containers, cover to prevent contamination
Store treated water in covered, clean containers away from sunlight

sanitation.title: Sanitation
items: Wash hands with soap and water when available, especially before eating
Dispose of waste away from water sources and living areas
Temporary toilet: dig a pit at least 30cm deep, 30m from water sources
Keep living areas dry and ventilated to reduce disease risk
Separate human waste from food preparation areas

first_aid.title: First Aid
items: Bleeding: apply direct pressure with clean cloth for at least 10 minutes
Burns: cool with clean running water for 20 minutes; do not apply ice
Dehydration: give small sips of clean water; seek medical help if severe
Shock: lay person flat, elevate legs if no injury; keep warm
This guide is not a substitute for professional medical care

shelter.title: Shelter Safety
items: Be aware of structural damage: cracks, leaning walls, unstable ceilings
Ensure adequate ventilation in enclosed spaces
Keep exits accessible; avoid overcrowding in small spaces
Do not enter visibly damaged buildings
This is general awareness — not an assessment of any specific location

no_power.title: No Electricity / No Fuel
items: Water purification: use chlorine tablets if available; sun exposure (SODIS) in clear bottles for 6+ hours
Keep food in coolest available place; eat perishables first
Avoid opening refrigerators unnecessarily if power is intermittent
Use LED or phone light sparingly to preserve battery
Share power banks and charging access with neighbours when safe

infant.title: Infant & Child Care
items: Breastfeeding is safest when possible — no clean water needed for formula preparation
If using formula: wash hands, use cleanest available water, prepare small amounts per feed
Watch for dehydration: fewer wet nappies, sunken fontanelle, lethargy — seek help urgently
Keep infants warm; hypothermia risk in unheated shelters
Continue routine vaccinations only when health services are confirmed accessible

chronic.title: Chronic Illness Without Medication
items: Do not stop essential medications abruptly without medical advice if any clinician is reachable
Keep a written list of medications and conditions for any health worker
Diabetes: monitor for confusion or extreme thirst; seek help if available
Epilepsy: protect from injury during seizures; do not restrain
This is awareness only — not a substitute for medical care

menstrual.title: Menstrual Hygiene
items: Use cleanest available cloth; wash and dry in sunlight if possible
Change materials frequently to reduce infection risk
Wash hands before and after changing materials
Dispose of waste discreetly away from water and living areas
Seek cloth or supplies from aid distributions when available

timeline.title: Information Timeline
timeline.subtitle: Chronological view of reported events and updates

sources.title: Data Sources
sources.subtitle: All data is attributed to original sources
sources.description: This platform aggregates publicly available humanitarian data. We do not modify the meaning of source reports.

list.ocha: UN OCHA — Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
list.rss: Verified RSS feeds from humanitarian agencies

sources.update_frequency: Data is updated every 15–30 minutes via automated pipeline

about.title: About & Ethics
about.mission: This platform displays verified humanitarian infrastructure, aggregates live news from trusted sources, provides general survival education, and shows information activity levels — without predicting events or designating safe zones.
dont_list: Safe zones or safe route recommendations
Claims that any location is safe or unsafe

pressure.title: Information Pressure
pressure.subtitle: Report volume and source diversity — NOT danger or safety levels
pressure.disclaimer: High activity means more reports are being published — it does not indicate increased risk or safety.

political.title: Political News Feed
political.subtitle: Attributed political and official statements — with transparent discourse metrics
political.disclaimer: Sentiment scores and the Discourse Intensity Quotient (DIQ) measure escalation-related LANGUAGE in tracked news headlines. They do NOT predict military action, assess personal risk, infer intent, or indicate that any location is safe or unsafe. High DIQ means more escalation-related words appear in reports — not that an attack is likely.
methodology_items: Articles are collected from attributed official statements and whitelisted news sources only.
Sentiment score (−100 to +100): counts predefined escalation vs. de-escalation terms in English headlines, weighted by recency (48-hour window).
Discourse Intensity Quotient (0–100): combines absolute language intensity and article volume — a media activity index, not a threat level.
No machine-learning prediction, no location scoring, no intent inference.
Keyword lists are published in source code for public audit.
Arabic headlines are displayed but scoring currently uses English text fields; translation pipeline planned.

metrics.sentiment_desc: Escalation vs. de-escalation language in tracked headlines. Published wording only.
metrics.diq_desc: Volume of escalation-related political discourse in tracked sources. NOT a threat forecast.
metrics.articles: Based on {{count}} articles in the last {{hours}}h

violence_safety.title: Safety During Violence
violence_safety.subtitle: General protective education aligned with ICRC/WHO conflict guidance — not location-specific advice
violence_safety.disclaimer: This is general educational information for conflict-affected populations. It does not identify safe locations, safe routes, or predict where violence may occur. Follow local emergency services when available.
violence_safety.sources_note: Content adapted from widely published humanitarian conflict safety principles (ICRC, WHO). Not a substitute for professional emergency response.

shelter.title: How to Take Shelter
items: Move away from windows, outer walls, and open spaces when explosions or strikes are reported nearby
Seek the lowest floor or an interior room with solid walls between you and the outside
Stay below window level; glass fragments cause many injuries
Lie flat on the ground if caught in the open; cover head and neck with hands or available cover
Do not assume any building is safe — this describes general principles only
If sheltering with others, stay calm and keep pathways clear for movement

vital_organs.title: Protecting Vital Organs
items: Lie face down with head turned to the side; hands over the back of the head and neck
Keep elbows tucked to protect chest and abdomen; knees slightly bent if space allows
Place a helmet, thick book, or bag over the head if available — partial protection only
Avoid running upright during active strikes; shrapnel travels horizontally
After blasts, check for bleeding, breathing difficulty, and shock; apply pressure to bleeding
Seek medical care as soon as it is safe to do so

protect_children.title: Protecting Children
items: Place infants and small children between adults and the wall, not near windows
Cover children's heads and bodies with your own body only as a last resort — your weight must not restrict breathing
Explain simply what is happening in age-appropriate language to reduce panic
Maintain physical contact — holding hands helps children feel secure
Keep a small bag ready with documents, water, and essential medicine if movement becomes necessary
Watch for signs of shock: pale skin, rapid breathing, confusion — keep warm and reassure

islamic.title: Islamic Guidance — Comfort & Resilience
islamic.subtitle: Spiritual support from Quranic and Prophetic teachings — complements professional care
islamic.disclaimer: This section offers Islamic spiritual and emotional support. It is not a substitute for professional mental health care, medical treatment, or emergency services. Seek qualified counsellors and imams in your community when possible.

soothe_children.title: Soothing Children
items: The Prophet (peace be upon him) showed gentleness to children — speak softly, reduce frightening details, and maintain routines where possible
Recite short surahs (Al-Fatiha, Al-Ikhlas, Al-Falaq, An-Nas) calmly — children find rhythm and voice comforting
Physical closeness is sunnah: holding, patting gently, and making du'a aloud together
Answer questions honestly but briefly; avoid graphic descriptions
Encourage drawing or play when safe — expression helps processing fear
Remind children that Allah is Ar-Rahman (The Most Merciful) and Al-Hafiz (The Protector) — age-appropriate tawakkul (trust in Allah)

cope_trauma.title: Coping with Trauma
items: Distress after violence is a normal human response — seeking help is strength, not weakness
Salah (prayer) and dhikr (remembrance) can anchor the heart; even shortened prayer counts when full prayer is difficult
The Prophet (peace be upon him) said: 'There is no disease that Allah has created, except that He also has created its treatment' (Bukhari) — this includes psychological care
Talk to trusted family or community members; isolation worsens trauma
Limit continuous news exposure — repeated trauma imagery deepens distress
Professional counselling is permissible and encouraged in Islam when distress persists

items.text: Hasbunallahu wa ni'mal wakeel — Allah is sufficient for us, and He is the best Disposer of affairs.
Allahumma inni a'udhu bika min al-hammi wal-hazan — O Allah, I seek refuge in You from worry and grief.
La hawla wa la quwwata illa billah — There is no power nor strength except with Allah.
Inna lillahi wa inna ilayhi raji'un — To Allah we belong and to Him we return.

community_support.title: Community & Family Support
items: Check on neighbours, especially elderly people and single mothers — sadaqah of time is highly valued
Share food and water where possible; the Prophet (peace be upon him) said the best of people are those most beneficial to others
Collective du'a after salah can unite hearts and reduce isolation
Protect dignity when helping — give privately when you can
Imams and qualified scholars can provide spiritual counsel; mental health professionals provide clinical care — both are valid
